title: US-led invasion of Afghanistan
summary: On the evening of 7 October 2001, less than a month after the al-Qaeda attacks on New York and Washington, the United States and the United Kingdom opened Operation Enduring Freedom with cruise-missile and bomber strikes on Taliban air-defence, command, and training targets across Afghanistan. Officially framed as retaliation for the September 11 attacks and the Taliban's refusal to surrender Osama bin Laden, the campaign combined heavy US and British air power with a small footprint of CIA paramilitary officers and Army Special Forces on the ground, working alongside the Northern Alliance under commanders such as Mohammad Fahim, Abdul Rashid Dostum, and the assassinated Ahmad Shah Massoud's successors. Within nine weeks the Taliban government had collapsed: Mazar-i-Sharif fell on 9 November, Kabul on 13 November, and Kandahar on 7 December 2001. A UN-sponsored conference in Bonn produced an interim administration under Hamid Karzai, sworn in on 22 December. The war that followed lasted almost two decades, cost, by the Costs of War Project's tally, more than 176,000 lives in Afghanistan and Pakistan by 2021, and ended with the Taliban's return to Kabul on 15 August 2021.

sections.heading: Background

image.caption: Official portrait of US President George W. Bush, who ordered the invasion on 7 October 2001.

sections.body:
Afghanistan on the eve of the American invasion was governed, in effect, by two rival authorities. The Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan, proclaimed by the Taliban movement of Mullah Mohammed Omar after their capture of Kabul in September 1996, controlled roughly nine-tenths of the country and had imposed one of the most austere interpretations of Sunni Islamic law seen in the modern world. Only three states, Pakistan, Saudi Arabia, and the United Arab Emirates, recognised the Emirate diplomatically. The remaining sliver of Afghan territory, in the Panjshir valley and parts of the northeast, was held by the United Islamic Front for the Salvation of Afghanistan, better known in the West as the Northern Alliance, a loose coalition of Tajik, Uzbek, and Hazara commanders operating under the political umbrella of the ousted Rabbani government and the military leadership of the Tajik commander Ahmad Shah Massoud.

Since the Soviet withdrawal of 1989 and the collapse of the pro-communist Najibullah government in 1992, Afghanistan had been in continuous civil war. The Taliban's rise had brought a certain security to areas it controlled but also mass expulsion of women from education and public life, the destruction in March 2001 of the sixth-century Buddha statues of Bamiyan, and, above all, sanctuary for the Saudi militant Osama bin Laden and the transnational al-Qaeda network. Bin Laden had returned to Afghanistan from Sudan in 1996 and, from camps around Kandahar, Khost, and Jalalabad, financed and directed a series of escalating attacks on American targets, including the 1998 bombings of the US embassies in Nairobi and Dar es Salaam and the October 2000 attack on the USS Cole in Aden harbour.

On 9 September 2001, two Tunisian al-Qaeda operatives posing as journalists detonated a suicide bomb concealed in a camera during an interview with Ahmad Shah Massoud in the village of Khwaja Bahauddin. Massoud died of his wounds within an hour, depriving the Northern Alliance of its most capable field commander two days before al-Qaeda hijackers flew four passenger aircraft into the World Trade Center, the Pentagon, and a Pennsylvania field. The 11 September attacks killed 2,977 people. President George W. Bush, in an address to Congress on 20 September, demanded that the Taliban hand over bin Laden and dismantle the al-Qaeda training camps; Mullah Omar refused without evidence of bin Laden's guilt. Planning for military action had already begun at Central Command under General Tommy Franks, and by the end of September CIA teams codenamed 'Jawbreaker' were on the ground in the Panjshir with cash, satellite phones, and orders to prepare the Northern Alliance for a joint offensive.

sections.heading: What happened

image.caption: US Air Force combat controller Bart Decker on horseback with Northern Alliance forces in northern Afghanistan, October 2001. Small special-operations teams like this called in the airstrikes that broke the Taliban front lines.

sections.body:
Operation Enduring Freedom opened at about 20:57 local time on the evening of 7 October 2001 with roughly fifty Tomahawk cruise missiles fired from US and British warships and submarines in the Arabian Sea, followed by strike sorties from long-range B-2 and B-52 bombers flying from Diego Garcia and the continental United States, and from carrier-based F-14s and F/A-18s of the USS Enterprise and USS Carl Vinson battle groups. The initial targets were Taliban air-defence radars, command posts, airfields near Kabul, Kandahar, Herat, Jalalabad, and Mazar-i-Sharif, and a handful of al-Qaeda training camps. In simultaneous televised addresses, Bush and British Prime Minister Tony Blair announced the strikes; Bush framed the campaign as one against 'terrorists and those who harbour them'. Fourteen other NATO and Commonwealth countries eventually contributed forces.

What distinguished the campaign from previous American wars was the ratio of firepower to boots. On the ground the initial US force consisted of no more than a few hundred CIA officers and Army Special Forces Operational Detachment-Alpha teams, of whom the most famous, ODA 595 under Captain Mitch Nelson, rode into battle on horseback with the forces of Uzbek commander Abdul Rashid Dostum on the plains south of Mazar-i-Sharif. Attached Air Force combat controllers used laser designators and satellite radios to guide precision munitions from B-52s circling at 35,000 feet onto Taliban trenches, tanks, and command bunkers. The tactic was devastating. On 9 November Dostum, Atta Muhammad Nur, and Mohammed Mohaqiq rode into Mazar-i-Sharif; Taliban resistance in the north collapsed within days. Kabul fell without a fight on 13 November as Taliban fighters retreated southward under cover of darkness. Jalalabad was abandoned on 14 November. Kunduz, the last northern stronghold, surrendered after a nine-day siege on 26 November amid controversy over the airlift of Pakistani intelligence officers and Taliban fighters out of the encirclement.

Only Kandahar, the Taliban's spiritual capital, and the Tora Bora cave complex in the White Mountains near the Pakistani border remained. US Marines from the 15th and 26th Marine Expeditionary Units, inserted by helicopter to a desert airstrip codenamed Rhino on 25 November, cut Taliban supply lines from the south. Kandahar fell on 7 December after negotiations between Hamid Karzai, a Pashtun tribal leader who had returned covertly from Pakistan, and Taliban commanders willing to surrender the city in exchange for amnesty. Mullah Omar slipped away on a motorbike. Between 3 and 17 December, US and British special forces backed local Afghan militias in an assault on the Tora Bora complex, believed to shelter Osama bin Laden and several hundred of his most senior fighters. The commander of the operation, CIA field officer Gary Berntsen, and Special Forces officers on the ground later argued that a request for a battalion of US Army Rangers to block the mountain passes to Pakistan was denied by Central Command. Bin Laden escaped, most likely into Pakistan's tribal areas, where he remained until his killing by US Navy SEALs in Abbottabad on 2 May 2011.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The military phase of the invasion had lasted just over two months. Its political sequel was arranged not in Afghanistan but in Germany. From 27 November to 5 December 2001, a UN-sponsored conference at the Hotel Petersberg outside Bonn brought together representatives of the Northern Alliance, exiled Rome-based supporters of the former king Zahir Shah, and two smaller Afghan diaspora factions. The Bonn Agreement, signed on 5 December, established an interim administration of thirty ministers under the chairmanship of Hamid Karzai, provided for a Loya Jirga to convene within six months to choose a transitional government, and authorised the deployment of an International Security Assistance Force, ISAF, initially in Kabul only. Karzai was sworn in on 22 December 2001. Notably absent from Bonn were representatives of the Taliban, a decision that a number of former UN officials, including special representative Lakhdar Brahimi, would later describe as the original strategic error of the intervention.

In the years that followed, ISAF expanded across the country under NATO command from 2003, eventually numbering more than 130,000 troops from over fifty nations at its 2011 peak. The reconstruction effort was vast in scale and thin in effect: the Special Inspector General for Afghanistan Reconstruction (SIGAR) later estimated that the United States alone spent about 145 billion dollars on reconstruction between 2002 and 2021, much of it lost to corruption, poor design, or physical destruction. The Taliban, regrouped in sanctuaries in Pakistan's Balochistan and Federally Administered Tribal Areas, began a sustained insurgency from around 2005 that gradually reclaimed rural districts across the south and east. Presidential elections in 2004, 2009, and 2014 produced governments increasingly disconnected from the countryside; the 2014 vote was so disputed that US Secretary of State John Kerry brokered a power-sharing 'National Unity Government' between the rival claimants Ashraf Ghani and Abdullah Abdullah.

Human costs were staggering. The Brown University Costs of War Project estimated in 2021 that approximately 176,000 people had died in the Afghanistan and Pakistan war zone since 2001, including some 46,000 Afghan civilians, 69,000 Afghan military and police, 51,000 Taliban and other opposition fighters, 2,461 American service members, and 1,144 troops from allied nations. Millions were displaced internally or into refugee populations in Iran and Pakistan. In February 2020 the Trump administration signed an agreement with the Taliban in Doha committing US and coalition forces to a complete withdrawal by May 2021. President Joe Biden extended the deadline to 31 August. The Afghan National Army disintegrated far faster than US planners had anticipated: Kabul fell to the Taliban on 15 August 2021, and the last American transport plane left Hamid Karzai International Airport just before midnight on 30 August, ending America's longest war.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The invasion of Afghanistan remains one of the most historically consequential decisions of the young twenty-first century, and its balance sheet is still being written. In the immediate short term it achieved its narrowest stated objectives: the al-Qaeda training infrastructure in Afghanistan was destroyed, the Taliban government was removed from power, and, after a decade of pursuit, Osama bin Laden was tracked down and killed. In a wider sense, however, the effort to remake Afghanistan into a functioning, centralised, Western-aligned state failed comprehensively. The August 2021 return of the Taliban, whose second Emirate has reinstated many of the same restrictions on women, education, and expression that had prompted international outrage a generation earlier, made the reversal impossible to disguise.

Inside the United States the war reshaped the machinery of government. The 2002 creation of the Department of Homeland Security, the passage of the USA PATRIOT Act, the expansion of National Security Agency signals-collection programmes disclosed by Edward Snowden in 2013, and the emergence of a global drone-strike apparatus operated from bases in Nevada, Djibouti, and elsewhere were all direct or indirect legacies of the campaign that began on 7 October 2001. The war reshaped American military doctrine towards counterinsurgency and, along with the 2003 invasion of Iraq, produced a generation of veterans with distinctive medical and social burdens: elevated rates of post-traumatic stress, traumatic brain injury from improvised explosive devices, and, controversially, a suicide rate among post-9/11 veterans that by the Costs of War Project's estimate had exceeded the combat death toll several times over by 2021.

Internationally, the invasion set the pattern for a two-decade era of Western liberal-interventionist wars, from Iraq in 2003 to Libya in 2011, whose repeated failures to produce stable post-war orders eroded the diplomatic confidence that had characterised the immediate post-Cold War moment. For Afghans, the war brought a temporary flowering of civil society, a whole generation of schoolgirls able to attend classes for the first time, a free press, and a professionalising civil service, before the collapse of 2021 forced tens of thousands into exile. The most enduring imagery of the war may prove to be the C-17 transports lifting off from Kabul with desperate Afghans clinging to their landing gear, a scene that, more than any speech or agreement, marked the end of the American project in Afghanistan.

figures.name: George W. Bush
figures.role: 43rd President of the United States; ordered Operation Enduring Freedom and framed the response to 9/11 as a global 'War on Terror'
figures.credit: Eric Draper / White House / Public domain

figures.name: Donald Rumsfeld
figures.role: US Secretary of Defense (2001-2006); champion of the light-footprint, special-operations-plus-airpower model used in the invasion

figures.name: Tommy Franks
figures.role: US Army general commanding US Central Command; operational architect of the invasion campaign

figures.name: Mullah Mohammed Omar
figures.role: Founder and Amir al-Mu'minin of the Taliban Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan; refused US demand to surrender Osama bin Laden and escaped Kandahar on 7 December 2001

figures.name: Osama bin Laden
figures.role: Saudi founder of al-Qaeda; principal target of the invasion, evaded capture at Tora Bora in December 2001 and was killed in Abbottabad, Pakistan, on 2 May 2011

figures.name: Ahmad Shah Massoud
figures.role: Tajik commander of the Northern Alliance ('Lion of Panjshir'); assassinated by al-Qaeda operatives posing as journalists on 9 September 2001, two days before the New York attacks

figures.name: Hamid Karzai
figures.role: Pashtun tribal leader chosen at the Bonn conference as chairman of the interim Afghan administration in December 2001; later first elected president of Afghanistan (2004-2014)

images.caption: US Air Force combat controller Bart Decker rides with Northern Alliance fighters in northern Afghanistan, October 2001. Small special-operations teams, mounted alongside anti-Taliban militias, called in the airstrikes that unlocked the Taliban front.

sources.title: How the US lost the longest war in Afghanistan
Human and Budgetary Costs to Date of the U.S. War in Afghanistan, 2001-2022
sources.publisher: Costs of War Project, Brown University
